summary: High‑energy Tower Theatre show with standout Lazy Lightning>Supplication
blurb: The first set features tight takes on Sugaree, Mama Tried and a fiery Lazy Lightning→Supplication segue. The second set brings St Stephen, Cosmic Charlie and an exuberant Wheel, ending with Johnny B. Goode. Overall the performance is energetic with strong highlights throughout.
review: June 23, 1976 at the Tower Theatre captured one of the Dead’s high‑octane midsummer nights. The band kicks off with a crisp “Sugaree” and a heartfelt “Mama Tried,” then rolls into a seamless Lazy Lightning→Supplication sequence that many listeners cite as a show‑highlight. The second set is a rollercoaster: while some tape sources note a dip in momentum, the standout moments—“St Stephen” weaving into “The Wheel,” a soaring “Cosmic Charlie,” and a rousing “Johnny B. Goode”—keep the energy alive. Across the three available recordings the audience tapes stand out for their clarity; one even boasts crystal‑clear vocals and a balanced Jerry tone that lets every instrument breathe. Even with occasional flat spots, the setlist’s flow and tight playing make this a memorable snapshot of the 1976 Dead on the road.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Two recordings are strongly positive, praising high energy, standout sequences (Lazy Lightning>Supplication, St Stephen, Wheel) and tight playing; one recording is mixed but only notes a flat second set and a single weak song (St Stephen). The predominance of praise yields a solid positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Clear, intimate audience tape that captures the high‑energy performance and highlights like Lazy Lightning→Supplication with minimal drops.

key_highlights: Stellar Lazy Lightning > Supplication segue that drives the first set.
High‑energy renditions of St. Stephen, Cosmic Charlie, and The Wheel in the second set.
Crystal‑clear audience recording delivers balanced mix and tight playing throughout.
Strong first‑set numbers such as Sugaree, Mama Tried, and Big River.

band_performance.Jerry: Guitar tone is balanced and sits well in the mix, letting vocals shine without overpowering.
